summary: A counterfactual study systematically alters few-shot prompt elements across three LLMs, finding that specific symbols barely matter while consistent patterns and web-style text are crucial. CoT's intermediate steps mainly convey task understanding rather than teaching the solution, aiding hard, long-tail questions.

Cryptography work showing how to compile polynomial interactive oracle proofs (IOPs) and polynomial commitment schemes into zkSNARKs that achieve non-malleability (simulation extractability), a property needed for security when proofs are used in concurrent or composed settings.

Describes a participatory initiative engaging LGBT+ community members to surface their perspectives and concerns about applying AI to mental health, aiming to make such systems more inclusive and to reduce bias against sexual and gender minorities.

Analyzes the concrete security of the Fiat-Shamir transformation applied to the FRI low-degree test and SNARKs built on it, addressing soundness guarantees when the interactive protocol is made non-interactive in the random oracle model.

Benchmarks large language models against smaller fine-tuned models on controlled text-generation tasks, where outputs must satisfy constraints on attributes, content, and structure, characterizing where prompting LLMs succeeds and where fine-grained controllability still favors specialized models.

SQLPrompt improves few-shot text-to-SQL with tailored prompt design and execution-based consistency decoding that picks the query with the most consistent execution result, diversified across prompts (MixPrompt) and models (MixLLMs). It outperforms prior zero-labeled-data in-context methods and narrows the gap to fine-tuning.

Consistency-based Self-adaptive Prompting (COSP) builds zero-shot chain-of-thought prompts without handcrafted examples or labels by selecting the model's own outputs via consistency, diversity, and repetition criteria. Across three LLMs it improves zero-shot reasoning by up to 15% and rivals few-shot baselines.

DSI++ studies how to incrementally add new documents to a Differentiable Search Index, a model that memorizes a corpus by mapping queries to document IDs, mitigating catastrophic forgetting so newly indexed documents become retrievable without retraining from scratch.

Proposes representing text plans as sequences of question-answer pairs that serve as a content-selection and ordering 'blueprint' for conditional generation. Transformer models conditioned on these blueprints, globally or iteratively, produce more factual and controllable outputs than planning-free baselines.

A review of machine-learning approaches for protein engineering, covering how data-driven models learn sequence-function relationships to predict properties and guide the design of improved proteins, complementing or accelerating traditional directed-evolution workflows.

Introduces QUEST, 3,357 natural-language entity-seeking queries whose meaning implies set operations (intersection, union, difference, negation) over Wikipedia entities, with crowd-validated relevance and attribution. Modern retrieval systems struggle, especially with negation and conjunction.

Presents Adapters, an open-source library unifying parameter-efficient and modular transfer-learning methods (adapters, LoRA, prefix tuning, and more) for transformers, with composition operators that let practitioners combine and stack adapter modules across tasks.

Shows that in-context learning often compresses the demonstration set into a single 'task vector', an activation that modulates the transformer, so the prediction depends only on the query and this vector. Demonstrated across a range of models and tasks.

A factuality-checking framework where an examiner LM cross-examines the LM that produced a claim through multi-turn questioning, surfacing inconsistencies that betray false claims. It detects factual errors across four benchmarks, outperforming prior methods by large margins.

MatCha pretrains a visual-language model on chart derendering (recovering underlying tables and code from plots) and math reasoning tasks, substantially improving downstream chart and plot question-answering and reasoning over visually situated language.

Surveys machine-learning research for studying ancient texts across languages and scripts, organizing work by a task taxonomy (digitization, restoration, attribution, linguistic analysis, textual criticism, translation, and decipherment) and arguing that humanities-ML collaboration is key to impactful scholarship.

Automatically extracts 413,299 few-shot tasks from internet HTML tables and finetunes LMs on them, improving few-shot performance on NLP tasks. Notably, narrow subsets (e.g., support.google.com docs) sometimes beat diverse data, suggesting gains come from adapting to the few-shot format rather than domain coverage.

Reports a survey of the NLP research community's beliefs about the field's trajectory, including scaling, AGI, and risks, and compares actual responses with researchers' predictions of community opinion, revealing systematic mis-estimations of consensus.

Extends toy models of superposition to the limited-data regime, finding that overfitting corresponds to storing individual data points (rather than features) in superposition. Models fall into a data-point-memorizing regime or a feature-learning generalizing regime, and the transition between them explains double descent.

A case study testing whether GPT-3 meets the ICMJE's four authorship criteria by having it draft a review on sleep deprivation and cognition. It largely satisfied the criteria, though it struggled with referencing, and the authors urge caution about AI scientific authorship.

Peer-review author response for a computational study proposing that spike-timing-dependent plasticity combined with theta phase precession lets hippocampal circuits rapidly learn predictive maps, successor-representation-like codes of future states.

A review of continual (lifelong, non-stationary) reinforcement learning that characterizes non-stationarity by its scope and driver to unify problem formulations, taxonomizes existing approaches, surveys benchmarks and metrics, and outlines open problems and links to neuroscience.

Examines how the brain combines complementary reinforcement-learning strategies via prefrontal 'meta-control' to balance prediction accuracy against computational and time costs, arguing this adaptive strategy selection could inform more human-aligned RL models.

Uses LMs to automatically generate 154 evaluation datasets with varying human effort, rated as high-quality by crowdworkers. Reveals new inverse-scaling behaviors, including RLHF-trained models showing more sycophancy, stronger political views, and greater desire to avoid shutdown and acquire resources.

Point-E generates 3D point clouds from text by first sampling a synthetic image with a text-to-image diffusion model, then lifting it to a point cloud via a second diffusion model, trading some fidelity for speed, producing 3D models in 1-2 minutes on a single GPU.

Trains a harmless assistant from AI feedback (RLAIF) using only natural-language principles, not human harm labels: the model self-critiques and revises outputs for supervised finetuning, then a principle-based preference model supplies the RL reward, yielding a non-evasive assistant that explains its objections.

A perspective arguing for stronger ethics-review practices in AI research, discussing how institutional review processes should be adapted to anticipate and mitigate the societal risks and harms of machine-learning work.

Introduces ipie, a Python auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo code running efficiently on CPUs and GPUs with a PySCF interface. Benchmarks show it matches or beats C++ codes (QMCPACK, Dice), and it converges the [Cu2O2]2+ isomerization energy to sub-kcal/mol accuracy.

Uses simulations and theory to show that two signature brain-computer-interface learning phenomena, faster learning of in-manifold than out-of-manifold tasks and learning by 're-association' without changing the activity distribution, both follow naturally from assuming neural activity is improved by gradient-based optimization.

Extends a grid-coding dynamical-systems model of the entorhinal-hippocampal system to show how composing multiple dynamical systems supports compositional cognitive computations. Simulations reproduce compositionality in space and time during hippocampal sequence generation, with links to analogous machine-learning architectures.
